mūla.devanāgarī: ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं परिज्ञाता त्रिविधा कर्मचोदना | करणं कर्म कर्तेति त्रिविधः कर्मसङ्ग्रहः
mūla.iast: jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ parijñātā trividhā karmacodanā | karaṇaṃ karma karteti trividhaḥ karmasaṅgrahaḥ
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 18 (Mokṣa-Sannyāsa-Yoga (The Yoga of Liberation by Renunciation)), verse 18
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: । ज्ञानादीनां हि त्रयाणां संनिपाते हानोपादानादिप्रयोजनः सर्वकर्मारम्भः स्यात्। ततः पञ्चभिः अधिष्ठानादिभिः आरब्धं वाङ्मनः
कर्म क्रिया पुरुषव्यापाररूपा भावना तद्विषया चोदना प्रेरणा विधिरूपा शाब्दीभावनेत्यर्थः
। परिज्ञाता तस्याश्रयो भोक्तान्तःकरणोपाधिपरिकल्पितः। एतेषां त्रयाणां,सन्निपाते हि हानोपादानादिसर्वकर्मारम्भः स्यादत एतत्

word_by_word.grammar: 3rd person singular periphrastic future active verb

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If every impulse to act (jnana, jneya, parijnata) is itself constituted by the gunas, can the decision to renounce action be any less guna-determined — or does the verse quietly dissolve the agent who would renounce?

Shankara and Ramanuja both affirm the three-member structure, yet one uses it to dissolve the agent and the other to oblige the agent toward Bhagavan. What is at stake in whether the karta is an upadhi-fiction or a real qualified jiva?

The verse pairs karma-codana (the impulse) with karma-sangraha (the locus). What happens when the impulse is present but the locus is missing — for instance, in dreaming or in samadhi? Does karma arise without a karana?
Madhva's reading preserves both divine vidhi and jiva akartritva simultaneously. Is this coherent, or does it smuggle back an autonomous jiva through the back door of 'abhimanika kartritva'?
If codana, upadesa, and vidhi are all synonyms (Bhatta formula), does the Gita's own prescriptive speech act here turn on itself — is BG 18.18 itself a codana whose operation is also tri-guna?

The bhakti school (Shridhara) uses tri-guna pervasiveness to demonstrate nirguna atman's non-contact with action. The advaita-bhakti school (Madhusudana) uses the same argument to validate traiguṇya-vishaya vedah. Are these compatible conclusions or rival uses of the same premise?

Modern cognitive science maps jnana onto representation, jneya onto affordance, and parijnata onto the predictive self-model. Does this mapping illuminate, flatten, or category-error the verse's analytic?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before beginning any project, notice the three-part structure already in motion: the knowing ('I understand the task'), the known object ('this is what must be done'), and the apparent knower ('I am the one who will do it'). Shankara's point is that this entire structure is assembled by conditioning, not by an independent self. Catching it assembling itself — without trying to stop it — is the praktica equivalent of jnana-yoga in daily work.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Ramanuja's reading invites a professional reframe: the knowledge of what needs doing, the task itself, and you-the-qualified-doer are not a burden but a gift that allows kainkarya (loving service). Each morning's brief review of 'what is required of me today, by whom, with what means' is not managerial routine but the Vedic vidhi structure operating in you — so bring it the same reverence you would a ritual.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's insight applies to creative blocks: the feeling 'I cannot act' or 'I am not the real author of this' is not nihilism but precision. Your kartritva is abhimanika — real enough for accountability, hollow enough that you need not carry the full weight of outcomes. Act through the vidhi (the task structure before you) and let Hari carry the result.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's prasada-lens: the very fact that right now you have the knowledge, the object of action, and the capacity to act is not your achievement — it is sattva as prasada. Treat each aligned moment ('I know what to do, I have what I need, I am able') as a gift arriving, not a resource you built. Gratitude precedes effort.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Shridhara's layering of codana as both psychological impulse and Vedic injunction suggests: when you feel genuinely moved to act rightly, do not second-guess it as 'mere emotion.' The impulse, the object, and the capacity converging at once is the tri-guna machinery serving its function. Ride it cleanly, without attachment to being its author.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's cid-acid-granthi (the knot of awareness and matter) is the practical touchpoint: in any moment of decision, you are neither pure witness nor pure body — you are the knot. Recognizing this prevents both the error of over-claiming agency ('I alone did this') and the error of spiritual bypass ('nothing is really happening'). Work from the knot, aimed at untying it.
